critic_raw: I want to probe the standardization approach for continuous features. The design standardizes to 'zero mean and unit variance using training statistics.' For a time series with trend (demand growth over 3-5 years), the mean and variance computed from the 60% training period may differ substantially from the test period. This is especially true if there is a trend — the test period mean load may be higher than the training mean, making all test-period load values systematically positive after standardization. This systematic shift in standardized values is a form of distribution shift that affects both models, but the TFT's attention mechanism may handle this differently than gradient boosting.
defender_raw: Systematic positive shift in standardized test values due to demand growth is a form of distribution shift that represents a genuine deployment challenge. However, both models receive the same standardized inputs and must generalize to this shift. If the TFT handles distribution shift better due to attention, this IS an architectural advantage. The design includes 'sensitivity analysis: comparing test-set errors in the first and second halves' — if the second half of the test period shows worse performance due to increasing shift, this is captured. For load forecasting, demand growth trends are typically gradual (a few percent per year), so the standardization shift is small. The lagged load features (prior 24 and 168 hours) are the primary input — the model learns relative changes rather than absolute values, reducing the impact of mean shift.
adjudication_raw: One concern: standardization mean shift due to demand growth trend in test period. Addressed: both models face same shift; TFT handling shift better is an architectural advantage; lagged features reduce absolute value dependency; sensitivity analysis captures temporal performance trends. Verdict: defense_wins.
